category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Complex Analysis — Entire Functions and Singularities

questiontext.text: An entire function is a function that is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Analytic (holomorphic) on all of \(\mathbb{C}\)

Entire functions are analytic on the whole complex plane \(\mathbb{C}\). Examples: polynomials, \(e^z\), \(\sin z\), \(\cos z\), \(\sinh z\). Non-examples: \(1/z\) (pole at 0), \(\log z\) (branch cut), \(\Gamma(z)\) (poles at non-positive integers). By Liouville's theorem, a bounded entire function is constant.

answer.text: Analytic on a bounded domain only

questiontext.text: Liouville's theorem states that a bounded entire function must be:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Constant

Liouville: if \(f\) is entire and \(|f(z)|\leq M\) for all \(z\in\mathbb{C}\), then \(f\) is constant. Proof: by Cauchy's estimate \(|f'(z)|\leq M/R\to0\) as \(R\to\infty\), so \(f'=0\). Corollary: the fundamental theorem of algebra (every nonconstant polynomial has a root).

questiontext.text: A nonconstant entire function \(f\) has at \(z=\infty\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Either a pole or an essential singularity

If \(f\) is entire and nonconstant, then \(z=\infty\) is either a pole (if \(f\) is a polynomial) or an essential singularity (if \(f\) is transcendental, e.g., \(e^z\)). By Picard's theorem, a transcendental entire function takes every value infinitely often with at most one exception.

answer.text: Either a pole (if polynomial) or an essential singularity (if transcendental)

questiontext.text: Picard's little theorem states: a nonconstant entire function \(f\) takes every complex value:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) With at most one exception

Little Picard: a nonconstant entire function omits at most one value. Example: \(e^z\) omits 0. Polynomials omit nothing (surjective). Great Picard: in any punctured neighbourhood of an essential singularity, \(f\) takes every value infinitely often with at most one exception. This contrasts with the Casorati–Weierstrass theorem (which only gives density).

answer.text: With at most one exception (infinitely often for all other values)
At most finitely many values are omitted

questiontext.text: If \(f\) is entire and \(\lim_{z\to0}|f(1/z)|=\infty\), then \(f\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) A nonconstant polynomial

\(\lim_{z\to0}|f(1/z)|=\infty\) means \(z=\infty\) is a pole of \(f\). Since \(f\) is entire, the only singularity can be at \(\infty\). An entire function with a pole at \(\infty\) is a polynomial. If it were transcendental, \(\infty\) would be an essential singularity, not a pole.

answer.text: An entire function with infinitely many zeros

questiontext.text: The Weierstrass factorization theorem states that every entire function with zeros \(a_1,a_2,\ldots\) (listed with multiplicity) can be written as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f(z)=z^m e^{g(z)}\prod_{n=1}^\infty E_{p_n}(z/a_n)\) where \(g\) is entire and \(E_p\) are elementary factors

Weierstrass factorization: every entire function factors as \(z^m e^{g(z)}\prod E_{p_n}(z/a_n)\), where the elementary factors \(E_p(u)=(1-u)\exp(u+u^2/2+\cdots+u^p/p)\) ensure convergence. For \(\sin\pi z=\pi z\prod_{n=1}^\infty(1-z^2/n^2)\) is a famous example.

questiontext.text: Casorati–Weierstrass theorem: in any deleted neighbourhood of an essential singularity \(z_0\), the image \(f(\{0<|z-z_0|<\delta\})\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Dense in \(\mathbb{C}\)

Casorati–Weierstrass: the image of any punctured neighbourhood of an essential singularity is dense in \(\mathbb{C}\). Example: near the essential singularity 0 of \(e^{1/z}\), values are dense in \(\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}\). Picard's theorem strengthens this: the image actually omits at most one point (not just "is dense").

questiontext.text: An entire function \(f\) that is not a polynomial:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) May have infinitely many zeros (or finitely many, or none)

A non-polynomial entire function (transcendental) can have any number of zeros: \(e^z\) has none; \(\sin z\) has infinitely many (\(z=n\pi\)); \(e^z-1\) has infinitely many (\(z=2\pi i n\)). By Hadamard's factorization, the zeros must satisfy \(\sum\frac{1}{|a_n|^{\rho+\epsilon}}<\infty\) for the order \(\rho\) of the function.

answer.text: Has exactly as many zeros as its degree
May have 0, finitely many, or infinitely many zeros depending on the function

questiontext.text: The order of an entire function \(f\) is \(\rho=\limsup_{r\to\infty}\frac{\ln\ln M(r)}{\ln r}\) where \(M(r)=\max_{|z|=r}|f(z)|\). The order of \(f(z)=e^{z^2}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 2

For \(f(z)=e^{z^2}\): \(M(r)=e^{r^2}\), so \(\ln M(r)=r^2\), \(\ln\ln M(r)=\ln(r^2)=2\ln r\). Then \(\frac{\ln\ln M(r)}{\ln r}=2\to2\). Order = 2. Generally, order of \(e^{z^n}\) is \(n\); order of a polynomial is 0.

questiontext.text: The maximum modulus principle states: if \(f\) is analytic and nonconstant on a connected open set \(D\), then \(|f|\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Cannot attain its maximum value in the interior of \(D\)

Maximum modulus principle: a nonconstant analytic function on a connected domain cannot have a local maximum of \(|f|\) at an interior point. The maximum of \(|f|\) on a compact set is attained on the boundary. This is a consequence of the mean value property of harmonic functions (Re\,f and Im\,f are harmonic).

answer.text: Cannot attain its maximum in the interior of \(D\)
Must attain its maximum at the center of \(D\)
Attains its maximum at all points of \(D\)

questiontext.text: Every entire function has a Taylor series \(\sum_{n=0}^\infty a_n z^n\) that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Converges for all \(z\in\mathbb{C}\) (radius of convergence is \(\infty\))

An entire function is analytic on all of \(\mathbb{C}\), so its Taylor series centered at 0 has infinite radius of convergence. Conversely, a power series with infinite radius of convergence represents an entire function. Examples: \(e^z=\sum z^n/n!\), \(\sin z=\sum(-1)^n z^{2n+1}/(2n+1)!\).

questiontext.text: The open mapping theorem states: a nonconstant analytic function on a connected open set maps:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Open sets to open sets

Open mapping theorem: if \(f\) is analytic and nonconstant on a connected open set \(D\), then \(f(D)\) is open in \(\mathbb{C}\). This fails for real analytic functions (\(f(x)=x^2\) maps \((-1,1)\) to \([0,1)\), not open). This theorem, combined with maximum modulus, shows entire functions cannot be bounded unless constant.

answer.text: Compact sets to compact sets only

name.text: Entire Functions - Zeros of Entire Function

questiontext.text: If \(f\) is entire and \(f(z)\neq0\) for all \(z\in\mathbb{C}\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f(z)=e^{g(z)}\) for some entire function \(g\)

If an entire function has no zeros, it is of the form \(e^{g(z)}\) for some entire \(g\). This follows from the Weierstrass factorization theorem (empty product). Conversely, \(e^{g(z)}\) is always nonzero. Combined with Liouville: if bounded and zero-free, then constant (but also expressible as \(e^c\)).

questiontext.text: The function \(f(z)=\sin z\) is an entire function of order 1 with zeros at \(z=n\pi\). Its Hadamard product is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\sin z = z\prod_{n=1}^\infty\left(1-\frac{z^2}{n^2\pi^2}\right)\)

Hadamard factorization of \(\sin z\): \(\sin z=z\prod_{n=1}^\infty\left(1-\frac{z^2}{n^2\pi^2}\right)\). This is the Weierstrass product (elementary factors of order 1 applied to \(\sin z\)). Comparing coefficients gives the Basel formula: \(\sum1/n^2=\pi^2/6\).

questiontext.text: The mean value property of analytic functions states: \(f(a)=\dfrac{1}{2\pi}\displaystyle\int_0^{2\pi}f(a+re^{i\theta})\,d\theta\). This implies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) The value at the center equals the average over any circle

This is Gauss's mean value theorem: for analytic \(f\), the value at the center of a circle equals the average value on the circle. It's a consequence of Cauchy's integral formula. Since real and imaginary parts are harmonic, harmonic functions also satisfy this property.

answer.text: The value at the center equals the average over any circle inside the domain
\(f\) achieves its minimum at the center
The integral of \(f\) over every circle is zero

questiontext.text: Cauchy's estimate for derivatives: if \(f\) is analytic on \(|z-a|\leq R\) with \(|f|\leq M\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(|f^{(n)}(a)|\leq\dfrac{n!\,M}{R^n}\)

From the \(n\)-th derivative formula of Cauchy: \(f^{(n)}(a)=\frac{n!}{2\pi i}\oint\frac{f(z)}{(z-a)^{n+1}}dz\). Estimating: \(|f^{(n)}(a)|\leq\frac{n!}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{M}{R^{n+1}}\cdot2\pi R=\frac{n!\,M}{R^n}\). This is used to prove Liouville's theorem and analyticity of convergent power series.

name.text: Entire Functions - f entire with f(z)=f(z+1)

questiontext.text: If \(f\) is an entire function satisfying \(f(z)=f(z+1)\) for all \(z\in\mathbb{C}\), and also \(|f(z)|\leq e^{2\pi|\mathrm{Im}\,z|}\), then \(f\) must be:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) A trigonometric polynomial (linear combination of \(e^{2\pi i n z}\))

A function periodic with period 1 satisfying the given growth bound has a Fourier series \(f(z)=\sum_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}c_n e^{2\pi inz}\). The growth condition \(|f|\leq e^{2\pi|\text{Im}\,z|}\) means \(c_n=0\) for \(|n|>1\), giving \(f(z)=c_{-1}e^{-2\pi iz}+c_0+c_1e^{2\pi iz}\).

questiontext.text: Hurwitz's theorem: if \(f_n\to f\) uniformly on compact subsets of a domain \(D\), each \(f_n\) has no zeros in \(D\), then \(f\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Either has no zeros in \(D\), or is identically zero

Hurwitz: if \(f_n\) are analytic with no zeros on a connected domain and \(f_n\to f\) uniformly on compact sets, then \(f\) either has no zeros or \(f\equiv0\). This is proved using Rouché's theorem and the argument principle. It's used to show limits of injective analytic functions are injective or constant.

answer.text: Either has no zeros in \(D\), or is identically zero on \(D\)

name.text: Entire Functions - Infinite Product for e^z

questiontext.text: Which of the following is an entire function with simple zeros at \(z=1,2,3,\ldots\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\dfrac{1}{\Gamma(1-z)}\) (reciprocal of Gamma function shifted)

The function \(1/\Gamma(1-z)\) is entire (since \(\Gamma\) has poles at non-positive integers, \(1/\Gamma(1-z)\) has zeros at \(z=1,2,3,\ldots\)). By Weierstrass: \(\frac{1}{\Gamma(z)}=ze^{\gamma z}\prod_{n=1}^\infty(1+z/n)e^{-z/n}\). This is a famous Weierstrass product for the gamma function.

questiontext.text: Let \(f\) be an entire function with \(|f(z)|\leq|z|^5+7\) for all \(z\). Then \(f\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) A polynomial of degree \(\leq5\)

Since \(|f(z)|\leq|z|^5+7\leq C(1+|z|^5)\) for large \(|z|\), the function \(f\) has at most polynomial growth. By a generalization of Liouville's theorem (using Cauchy's estimates on \(f^{(6)}\)): \(|f^{(6)}(z)|\leq\frac{6!\,C(1+R^5)}{R^6}\to0\) as \(R\to\infty\). So \(f^{(6)}\equiv0\), meaning \(f\) is a polynomial of degree \(\leq5\).
